items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the rediscovery of perspective in early Renaissance painting
items.transcript: Take a look at this fresco from around 1300. Notice how flat it feels, how the figures seem to float without any real depth behind them. For centuries, European painters had essentially lost the ability to show space convincingly. So what changed? In the early fifteenth century, artists in Florence rediscovered linear perspective, a mathematical system for representing three-dimensional space on a flat surface. The key figure here is the architect Filippo Brunelleschi. Around 1420, he conducted a famous demonstration. He painted the Florence Baptistery on a small wooden panel, drilled a tiny hole through the center, and had viewers look through the back while holding a mirror up in front of it. When they compared the reflection to the actual building, the painted version matched the real one almost perfectly. The trick was the vanishing point, a single point on the horizon where parallel lines appear to converge. Once artists grasped this principle, painting transformed. The painter Masaccio applied it in his fresco of the Holy Trinity. Suddenly the painted chapel looked like a real architectural space you could walk right into. Now, it's important to understand what this was not. The ancient Romans had used some intuitive depth effects in their wall paintings, but they never formalized the underlying geometry. The Renaissance achievement was making it systematic and repeatable, something any trained painter could learn and reproduce. And that's exactly why perspective spread so quickly across Europe. Within a single generation, it became the very foundation of Western painting, shaping how artists composed their images for the next five hundred years.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Renaissance artists developed a systematic method for depicting space
options.B: The life and architectural career of Filippo Brunelleschi
options.C: Why ancient Roman wall paintings looked flat and shallow
options.D: How a mirror can be used to copy a real building accurately

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括了整篇讲座的主旨——文艺复兴画家重新发现并系统化了线性透视这一表现空间的方法。B项（too_narrow）只聚焦于Brunelleschi一个人，而他只是讲座中的一个例子，并非主题。C项（wrong_topic）讲的是罗马壁画，讲座只是顺带提及罗马人作对比，并非主要内容。D项（surface_word_trap）借用了讲座中‘镜子’这个词，但只是Brunelleschi演示中的一个细节，被误导性地当成主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is the vanishing point?

options.A: The tiny hole that Brunelleschi drilled through his panel
options.B: A point on the horizon where parallel lines appear to meet
options.C: The spot where a viewer stands to hold up the mirror
options.D: The center of the fresco painted by the artist Masaccio

questions.explanation: 正确答案B直接复述讲座的定义——消失点是地平线上平行线看似汇聚的一个点。A项（wrong_detail）把消失点和Brunelleschi钻的小孔混为一谈，二者是不同的东西。C项（topic_adjacent）听起来与演示相关，但讲座从未说消失点是观看者站立的位置。D项（reversed_logic/wrong_detail）把消失点错误地说成是Masaccio壁画的中心，混淆了概念与具体作品。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Brunelleschi's demonstration with the panel and mirror?

options.A: To suggest that Brunelleschi was a better painter than Masaccio
options.B: To explain how mirrors were manufactured in Renaissance Florence
options.C: To show how convincingly the new method could reproduce reality
options.D: To argue that architecture mattered more than painting at the time

questions.explanation: 正确答案C点明了教授描述这个演示的目的——通过画作与真实建筑几乎完美吻合，证明新方法能多么逼真地再现现实。A项（wrong_purpose）误把目的说成比较两位画家的优劣，讲座没有此意。B项（wrong_purpose）扯到镜子的制造工艺，完全偏离演示的用意。D项（over_inference）强加了一个教授未表达的论点，即建筑比绘画更重要。

questions.stem: Why does the professor point out what the Roman painters did not do?

options.A: To claim that Roman wall paintings have all been lost over time
options.B: To suggest that the Romans invented linear perspective first
options.C: To restate that the Romans used depth effects in their paintings
options.D: To emphasize that the Renaissance contribution was a formal system

questions.explanation: 正确答案D指出教授提到罗马人‘没做什么’是为了强调文艺复兴的真正贡献在于把透视变成了一套可重复的正式几何系统。A项（wrong_purpose）说罗马壁画都已失传，讲座根本没提及这一点。B项（reversed_logic）颠倒了逻辑，教授恰恰说罗马人‘从未’把几何系统化，而非发明了它。C项（literal_restatement）只是复述‘罗马人用了深度效果’这一事实内容，而没有解释教授提及它的用意。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: how invasive species disrupt native food webs
items.transcript: Imagine a lake where the fish, the plants, and the birds have all lived in balance for thousands of years. Then one new species arrives, and within a few seasons the whole system starts to fall apart. Today I want to talk about invasive species and how they disrupt native food webs. An invasive species is an organism that's introduced into a new environment, usually by humans, where it has no natural predators. Without anything to keep its numbers in check, it can multiply rapidly and outcompete the native species for food and space. Let me give you a clear example: the zebra mussel in the Great Lakes. Zebra mussels arrived in the 1980s in the ballast water of ships coming over from Europe. Each mussel filters the water to feed on tiny floating plants called plankton. That sounds harmless enough, but these mussels are incredibly efficient, and they reproduce by the millions. Together they stripped enormous amounts of plankton right out of the water. And here's the real problem. Plankton sits at the very base of the food web. The small native fish that feed on plankton suddenly had far less to eat, so their populations dropped sharply. And the larger fish that prey on those small fish declined too. So one tiny mussel sent shock waves all the way up the food chain. This is why ecologists worry so much about invasions. It isn't just about one new animal. Adding or removing a single species at the bottom can reshape the entire community living above it.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How ships accidentally carry mussels across the ocean in ballast water
options.B: How an introduced species can disrupt a native food web
options.C: Why plankton are the single most important organisms in any lake
options.D: How fishermen in the Great Lakes have adapted to fewer fish

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括主旨——一个外来物种如何扰乱整个本地食物网。A项（surface_word_trap）借用‘压舱水里的船’这一细节，但那只是斑马贻贝如何到达的过程，不是主题。C项（too_narrow）夸大了浮游生物的地位，讲座只说它处于食物网基础，并未说它是最重要的生物。D项（wrong_topic）扯到渔民如何适应，讲座完全没有讨论渔民。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what do zebra mussels feed on?

options.A: The small native fish living in the Great Lakes
options.B: The ballast water carried inside European ships
options.C: Tiny floating plants in the water called plankton
options.D: Larger predatory fish at the top of the food chain

questions.explanation: 正确答案C直接复述讲座内容——斑马贻贝以水中称为浮游生物的微小漂浮植物为食。A项（reversed_logic）颠倒了关系，是小鱼吃浮游生物，贻贝并不吃小鱼。B项（wrong_detail）把贻贝到达的途径（压舱水）误当成它的食物。D项（reversed_logic）说贻贝吃大型捕食鱼，方向完全相反，大鱼处于食物链顶端。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happened to the small native fish?

options.A: They began feeding directly on the zebra mussels instead
options.B: They multiplied quickly once the mussels removed competitors
options.C: They moved to deeper parts of the lake to find shelter
options.D: Their populations dropped because they had far less food

questions.explanation: 正确答案D复述讲座——因为浮游生物被贻贝大量过滤掉，小型本地鱼的食物锐减，种群随之下降。A项（topic_adjacent）说小鱼改吃贻贝，讲座从未提及。B项（reversed_logic）颠倒结果，小鱼数量是下降而非快速增多。C项（topic_adjacent）说小鱼游到更深处躲避，听似合理但讲座没有说。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about ecosystems?

options.A: A change at the food web's base can reshape the whole system
options.B: Native species are always stronger than newly arrived invaders here
options.C: Invasive species mainly harm the largest animals in a system
options.D: Most lakes recover their natural balance within a few short seasons

questions.explanation: 正确答案A是合理推断——教授强调底层一个物种的改变会震动整个食物链，暗示生态系统中底层变化能影响整个群落。B项（over_generalization）把‘本地物种总是更强’说得过于绝对，恰恰相反，外来种压倒了本地种。C项（reversed_logic）说外来种主要危害最大的动物，实际影响是从底层向上传导，并非只针对大动物。D项（topic_adjacent）说湖泊几季就能恢复平衡，讲座反而强调系统被重塑、难以恢复。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: how the Doppler effect changes the pitch of a passing sound
items.transcript: You've all heard this before. An ambulance races toward you with its siren wailing, and the very moment it passes by, the pitch of the siren suddenly drops. Why does that happen? The answer is something physicists call the Doppler effect. Sound travels as waves, a series of compressions moving outward through the air. The pitch you hear depends on how frequently those waves reach your ears. The more waves arrive per second, the higher the pitch sounds. Now here's the key idea. When the ambulance is moving toward you, each successive wave is emitted from a slightly closer position than the one before it. The waves get bunched together, arriving more frequently, so you hear a higher pitch. Once the ambulance passes and starts moving away, each wave is emitted from a slightly farther position. Now the waves get stretched out, arriving less frequently, and the pitch you hear drops. The important thing to notice is that the siren itself never actually changes. The ambulance is producing exactly the same tone the entire time it travels. What changes is the relationship between the source of the sound and you, the listener standing still. The same principle applies to anything that emits waves while moving, including light. And that turns out to be enormously useful. In our next class, we'll look at how astronomers apply the Doppler effect to light from distant galaxies. By measuring whether that light is shifted toward red or toward blue, they can tell whether a galaxy is racing away from us or moving toward us, and how fast.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How astronomers measure the distance to faraway galaxies
options.B: Why ambulance sirens are designed to be so loud and high
options.C: Why a moving source changes the pitch a listener hears
options.D: How sound waves travel as compressions through the open air

questions.explanation: 正确答案C抓住主旨——运动的声源为何会改变听者听到的音高，这正是多普勒效应的核心。A项（wrong_topic）讲天文学测距，那只是结尾预告的下节课内容，不是本讲主题。B项（surface_word_trap）借用‘救护车警笛响而高’的词面，但讲座并不讨论警笛为何设计得响亮。D项（too_narrow）只聚焦‘声波是压缩波’这一背景细节，并非整篇主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what determines the pitch a person hears?

options.A: The total distance the sound has traveled through the air
options.B: How loudly the source is producing the original tone
options.C: How far away the listener is standing from the source
options.D: How frequently the sound waves arrive at the listener's ears

questions.explanation: 正确答案D复述讲座——音高取决于声波到达耳朵的频率，每秒到达的波越多音高越高。A项（wrong_detail）说取决于声音传播的总距离，讲座并未这样说。B项（topic_adjacent）把音高与响度混淆，响度并不决定音高。C项（topic_adjacent）说取决于听者离声源多远，听似合理但讲座说决定因素是波到达的频率，而非静态距离。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to the sound waves as the ambulance moves away?

options.A: They get stretched out and arrive less frequently
options.B: They get bunched together and arrive more frequently
options.C: They speed up and reach the listener much sooner
options.D: They grow louder while keeping the very same pitch

questions.explanation: 正确答案A复述讲座——救护车远离时，每个波从更远处发出，波被拉长、到达频率降低。B项（reversed_logic）描述的是救护车驶近时的情形，方向相反。C项（wrong_detail）说波速加快、更快到达，讲座讲的是到达频率变化而非波速变化。D项（wrong_detail）说波变响但音高不变，混淆了响度与本题关于频率/音高的内容。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss in the next class?

options.A: How engineers reduce unwanted noise from passing vehicles
options.B: How astronomers use the Doppler effect on light from galaxies
options.C: How the human ear physically detects and separates pitches
options.D: How the speed of sound changes in different kinds of air

questions.explanation: 正确答案B直接对应结尾预告——下节课将讨论天文学家如何把多普勒效应用于来自遥远星系的光。A项（wrong_topic）扯到工程师降噪，讲座结尾没有提到。C项（too_narrow）聚焦人耳如何分辨音高，那是本讲内部的细节方向，而非预告的下一主题。D项（reversed_logic）说声速在不同空气中的变化，与预告的‘光的红移蓝移’内容不符。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how certain plants use mimicry to attract pollinators
items.transcript: Most flowers attract their pollinators with an honest reward: nectar or pollen. The insect gets a meal, the flower gets pollinated, and everyone comes out ahead. But some plants have evolved a far more cunning strategy. They cheat. Today we're looking at floral mimicry, where a plant imitates something else entirely in order to deceive a pollinator into visiting it. Consider the bee orchid. Part of its flower has evolved to look remarkably like a female bee, complete with the right coloring, a fuzzy texture, and even a scent that mimics the female's own chemical signals. A male bee, completely fooled by the resemblance, lands and attempts to mate with the flower. In the process, he picks up a packet of pollen, and when he tries again with another orchid nearby, he transfers it. The orchid gets pollinated without offering any reward at all. So why would such an elaborate deception evolve in the first place? Think about it from the plant's perspective. Producing nectar is genuinely costly. It takes energy and resources the plant could spend elsewhere. A plant that can attract pollinators without that expense has a real advantage, as long as enough insects keep falling for the trick. And that last condition is absolutely crucial. If too many males learn to avoid these flowers, the deception stops working. That's precisely why these orchids tend to be rare and widely scattered, never abundant in one place. The strategy only succeeds when the deceiver stays uncommon enough that the pollinators don't ever quite catch on.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How bees locate flowers by following floral scents
options.B: Why producing nectar is too costly for most flowering plants
options.C: How orchids around the world differ in shape and color
options.D: How some plants deceive pollinators instead of rewarding them

questions.explanation: 正确答案D概括主旨——某些植物通过欺骗而非给予奖励来吸引传粉者。A项（wrong_topic）说蜜蜂靠气味找花，讲座只把气味作为兰花拟态的一部分，并非主题。B项（too_narrow）只抓住‘产蜜成本高’这一支撑性细节，而非整篇主旨。C项（wrong_topic）说世界各地兰花的形状颜色差异，讲座并不做这种比较。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how does the bee orchid deceive a male bee?

options.A: Part of its flower resembles a female bee in look and scent
options.B: It releases a sweet nectar that male bees cannot resist
options.C: It produces a loud sound that imitates a female bee's wings
options.D: It changes its color to warn rival males to stay away

questions.explanation: 正确答案A复述讲座——兰花的一部分在外形和气味上都模拟雌蜂，从而骗过雄蜂。B项（reversed_logic）说兰花靠甜美花蜜吸引，恰恰相反，讲座强调它不提供任何奖励。C项（topic_adjacent）说兰花发出模拟翅膀的声音，讲座完全没提声音。D项（wrong_detail）说兰花变色驱赶对手，曲解了拟态的目的与方式。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say that producing nectar is genuinely costly?

options.A: To warn that the bee orchid may eventually run out of nectar
options.B: To explain why a deceptive strategy could be advantageous
options.C: To restate that nectar is made from energy and resources
options.D: To prove that honest flowers always outcompete deceptive ones

questions.explanation: 正确答案B点明用意——教授指出产蜜成本高，是为了解释为什么‘欺骗’这种不产蜜的策略反而可能更有优势。A项（wrong_purpose）说兰花会耗尽花蜜，可兰花根本不产蜜，曲解了用意。C项（literal_restatement）只是复述‘花蜜由能量和资源制成’这一内容本身，没有解释教授为何提及。D项（over_inference）强加一个相反的论断，即诚实的花总能胜过欺骗的花，教授并未如此主张。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that the orchids are rare and widely scattered?

options.A: To suggest that the orchids are now in danger of extinction
options.B: To restate simply that the deceptive orchids are not common
options.C: To show why the deception keeps working on the pollinators
options.D: To argue that rare plants always attract the most pollinators

questions.explanation: 正确答案C点明用意——教授提到兰花稀少且分散，是为了说明正因为骗子保持稀少，传粉者才不会识破，欺骗才得以持续奏效。A项（wrong_purpose）说兰花濒临灭绝，讲座并无此意，稀少是策略需要而非危机。B项（literal_restatement）只是把‘兰花不常见’这一事实换种说法复述，没有解释原因。D项（over_inference）强加一个过度的普遍论断，即稀有植物总能吸引最多传粉者，讲座并未这样说。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how chiaroscuro created depth in Baroque painting
items.transcript: Stand in front of a Baroque painting and you almost feel like you could step right into it. The figures seem to bulge out of the canvas, lit as if by a single candle in a dark room. That dramatic interplay of light and shadow has a name: chiaroscuro. The word comes from Italian, chiaro meaning light and scuro meaning dark. It refers to the technique of using strong contrasts between bright and dark areas to model the illusion of three-dimensional form on a flat surface. The Italian painter Caravaggio pushed this further than anyone before him, around the year 1600. Take his painting The Calling of Saint Matthew. A shaft of light cuts diagonally across a dim room, picking out a few faces and hands while the rest dissolves into deep brown shadow. Your eye is pulled exactly where Caravaggio wants it. He didn't paint a gentle, even daylight; he used darkness as an active tool. Now, here's what's worth noticing. Earlier Renaissance painters often spread light evenly to show every detail clearly. Caravaggio did the opposite. By hiding parts of the scene in shadow, he made the lit parts feel more solid, more urgent, more alive. So chiaroscuro isn't just about making things visible. It's about using what you can't see to give weight and drama to what you can.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: A painting technique that uses light and dark contrast to create depth
options.B: The biography and major commissions of the painter Caravaggio
options.C: How Italian art vocabulary entered everyday English usage
options.D: The religious subjects favored by Baroque-era artists

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是 chiaroscuro 这一明暗对比技法如何在平面上营造立体与纵深感，A 正确。B 只聚焦卡拉瓦乔的生平，属于 too_narrow，讲座只把他当例子。C 抓住了意大利词源这个细节并放大成主题，属 surface_word_trap。D 提到的宗教题材在讲座中并非重点，属 wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the word 'chiaroscuro' literally mean?

options.A: Shadow and form combined on a canvas
options.B: Light and dark
options.C: Bright candle in darkness
options.D: Three-dimensional illusion

questions.explanation: 教授明确说 chiaro 意为 light，scuro 意为 dark，所以字面意思就是 light and dark，B 正确。A 把 form 混进词义，改动了关键事实，属 wrong_detail。C 借用讲座中蜡烛的比喻，但那不是词义，属 topic_adjacent。D 是该技法要达到的效果，而非词的字面含义，属 reversed_logic（把结果当成定义）。

questions.stem: How does the professor describe earlier Renaissance painters' use of light?

options.A: They used a single diagonal shaft to highlight key figures
options.B: They left large areas dissolving into deep brown shadow
options.C: They often spread light evenly to show every detail clearly
options.D: They avoided depicting light sources within their scenes

questions.explanation: 教授说早期文艺复兴画家常把光线均匀铺开以清晰展现每个细节，C 正确。A 描述的是卡拉瓦乔的对角光束，张冠李戴，属 wrong_detail。B 同样是卡拉瓦乔的暗部处理，属 reversed_logic（与文艺复兴做法相反）。D 在讲座中从未提及，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the role of shadow in Caravaggio's work?

options.A: Shadow was used only to disguise unfinished areas of a painting
options.B: Shadow weakened the realism that lighter Renaissance styles achieved
options.C: Shadow was a minor element compared with his choice of subject matter
options.D: Shadow actively shaped the viewer's attention and heightened drama

questions.explanation: 教授强调卡拉瓦乔把黑暗当作主动工具，用看不见的部分给可见的部分增加分量和戏剧性，D 是合理推断。A 把暗部贬为掩盖未完成处，讲座毫无支持，属 topic_adjacent。B 与讲座相反，教授认为暗部反而让物体更真实立体，属 reversed_logic。C 把暗部说成次要，与教授强调其核心作用矛盾，属 over_generalization。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how tardigrades survive extreme conditions through cryptobiosis
items.transcript: There's an animal that can survive being boiled, frozen to nearly absolute zero, blasted with radiation, and even shot into the vacuum of space. And it's barely half a millimeter long. We call it the tardigrade, or sometimes the water bear. So how does something so tiny shrug off conditions that would kill almost anything else? The answer is a state called cryptobiosis. When a tardigrade's environment dries out, it doesn't just go to sleep. It essentially switches off its metabolism almost completely, expelling most of the water from its body and curling into a shriveled ball called a tun. In this tun state, the chemical reactions we associate with being alive nearly stop. Here's the key. As the water leaves, the tardigrade produces special proteins that some researchers think replace the missing water, forming a kind of glass-like matrix inside the cells. This rigid scaffold holds delicate structures in place so they don't tear apart. The animal can stay like this for years, maybe decades. Then add a drop of water, and within hours it rehydrates, the metabolism flickers back on, and it walks away as if nothing happened. Now, this matters beyond just a biological curiosity. If we can understand how these proteins protect cells during total dry-out, it could help us preserve vaccines or even human tissue without refrigeration. The tardigrade may be teaching us how to pause life itself.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How tardigrades survive extreme conditions by shutting down their metabolism
options.B: Why tardigrades are among the smallest animals ever discovered
options.C: How scientists transport vaccines safely without refrigeration
options.D: The discovery of new animal species in outer space

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是缓步动物通过 cryptobiosis 几乎关闭代谢从而在极端环境中存活，A 正确。B 抓住体型这个细节并夸大成主题，属 too_narrow。C 是讲座结尾提到的潜在应用，并非主旨，属 surface_word_trap。D 把太空当成发现新物种之地，纯属误读，属 wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is a 'tun'?

options.A: A special protein that replaces water inside the cells
options.B: A shriveled, water-expelled ball the tardigrade curls into
options.C: The vacuum chamber used to test tardigrade survival
options.D: A glass-like matrix formed in the surrounding environment

questions.explanation: 教授说缓步动物排出体内大部分水分、蜷成一个干瘪的球，称为 tun，B 正确。A 描述的是保护性蛋白，与 tun 不是同一概念，属 wrong_detail。C 是测试条件，讲座并未这样定义 tun，属 topic_adjacent。D 把玻璃状基质说成形成于外部环境，而讲座说它在细胞内，方向颠倒，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what role do the special proteins play?

options.A: They speed up the animal's metabolism during the dry-out
options.B: They draw additional water into the body from outside
options.C: They form a rigid scaffold that holds delicate structures in place
options.D: They emit radiation that destroys invading microorganisms

questions.explanation: 教授指出这些蛋白形成玻璃状刚性支架，把脆弱结构固定住以免撕裂，C 正确。A 与讲座相反，tun 状态下代谢几乎停止而非加快，属 reversed_logic。B 说蛋白从外界吸水，但讲座说水分是被排出、蛋白是替代水分，属 wrong_detail。D 凭空赋予蛋白抗辐射杀菌功能，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studying tardigrades?

options.A: It is mainly valuable as an interesting biological curiosity
options.B: It could lead to ways of preserving biological materials without cold storage
options.C: It proves that human tissue can already be revived after total dry-out
options.D: It suggests tardigrades originally evolved somewhere beyond Earth

questions.explanation: 教授说理解这些蛋白如何在彻底脱水时保护细胞，可能帮助我们在无需冷藏的情况下保存疫苗甚至人体组织，B 是合理推断。A 与教授强调‘意义远不止生物学奇观’相反，属 reversed_logic。C 把潜在前景夸张成已实现的事实，属 over_generalization。D 借太空这个意象编出外星起源，讲座毫无支持，属 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: how spectroscopy reveals the composition of distant stars
items.transcript: How can we possibly know what a star is made of when it sits trillions of kilometers away and no probe will ever reach it? We can't scoop up a sample. Yet astronomers will tell you, with confidence, that the Sun is mostly hydrogen and helium. The tool that makes this possible is spectroscopy. When you pass starlight through a prism or a diffraction grating, it spreads into a spectrum, a rainbow band. But it's not a smooth rainbow. Crossing it are thin dark lines, gaps where certain colors are missing. These are called absorption lines. Here's why they appear. Each chemical element absorbs light at its own specific set of wavelengths, like a fingerprint. As light from the star's hot interior passes through its cooler outer atmosphere, the atoms there absorb exactly those wavelengths, leaving dark gaps in the spectrum. So when astronomers see a dark line at the wavelength known for, say, sodium, they know sodium is present in that star. The pattern of lines tells us the chemistry. And it tells us more. The lines can be shifted slightly toward the red end of the spectrum if the star is moving away from us, which is how we measure a star's motion. So a single band of starlight, properly read, carries an astonishing amount of information, composition, temperature, even velocity, all encoded in lines of light and shadow. This is how we read the universe from our own backyard.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why no space probe can ever reach a distant star
options.B: How spectroscopy lets astronomers determine what stars are made of
options.C: How prisms and diffraction gratings are manufactured
options.D: Why the Sun contains mostly hydrogen and helium

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是光谱学如何让天文学家判断遥远恒星的成分（及更多信息），B 正确。A 只是开头抛出的引子，并非主题，属 too_narrow。C 把棱镜、光栅这些工具名拿来当主题，属 surface_word_trap。D 是讲座举的一个事实例子，而非整体话题，属 wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do dark absorption lines appear in a star's spectrum?

options.A: The prism blocks certain colors as light passes through it
options.B: The star's hot interior emits light only at a few wavelengths
options.C: Atoms in the star's cooler atmosphere absorb specific wavelengths
options.D: Distant starlight fades unevenly while crossing empty space

questions.explanation: 教授解释恒星较冷外层大气中的原子吸收特定波长的光，从而在光谱中留下暗线，C 正确。A 把成因归于棱镜，与讲座机制不符，属 wrong_detail。B 说热内层只发出少数波长的光，颠倒了机制（内层发出连续光，是外层吸收造成缺口），属 reversed_logic。D 用空间中光线衰减来解释，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what can a slight shift of the lines toward the red end indicate?

options.A: That the star contains an unusually large amount of sodium
options.B: That the prism was not properly aligned during the reading
options.C: That the star's outer atmosphere is hotter than its interior
options.D: That the star is moving away from us

questions.explanation: 教授明确说谱线向红端轻微移动表明恒星正在远离我们，由此可测其运动，D 正确。A 把红移误解成钠含量，张冠李戴，属 wrong_detail。B 归因于棱镜未校准，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。C 声称外层比内层热，与讲座所述外层较冷相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a single band of starlight?

options.A: It can reveal multiple properties of a star at once when read correctly
options.B: It provides reliable data only for stars relatively close to Earth
options.C: It must be combined with a physical sample to be trustworthy
options.D: It reveals a star's chemistry but nothing about its movement

questions.explanation: 教授说一束被正确解读的星光能同时承载成分、温度甚至速度等大量信息，A 是合理推断。B 加了‘只对近距离恒星可靠’这一讲座未给的限制，属 over_generalization。C 与讲座相反，光谱法正是无需实物样本，属 reversed_logic。D 否认能测运动，但讲座明确说红移可测速度，属 topic_adjacent（与事实相悖的相关推断）。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: how the bystander effect reduces individual willingness to help
items.transcript: Imagine someone collapses on a crowded subway platform. You'd think that with dozens of people around, help would come fast. But research suggests the opposite can happen. The more people present, the less likely any one of them is to step in. Psychologists call this the bystander effect. The core idea is something called diffusion of responsibility. When you're the only witness to an emergency, the responsibility to act falls entirely on you. But when there's a crowd, that responsibility gets spread thin. Each person assumes someone else will handle it, and so, often, no one does. Researchers Latané and Darley studied this in the late 1960s. In one experiment, participants filled out forms in a room when smoke began seeping in under the door. Alone, most people quickly got up to report it. But when they sat with others who calmly ignored the smoke, far fewer reacted. They looked around, saw no one alarmed, and assumed it must be nothing. There's a second factor at work here, then. We take cues from others about how to interpret a situation. If everyone looks calm, we tell ourselves there's no real emergency. So the bystander effect isn't simply about people being callous or indifferent. It's about how the presence of others quietly reshapes our sense of personal responsibility and even our reading of reality itself.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How to perform first aid correctly in a crowded public space
options.B: Why people tend to feel calm and safe inside large crowds
options.C: How the presence of others reduces an individual's willingness to help
options.D: Why smoke-detection systems are required in public buildings

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是旁观者效应——他人在场会降低个体出手相助的意愿，C 正确。A 把话题误读成急救操作，属 wrong_topic。B 抓住‘人群’与‘冷静’这两个词拼出错误主题，属 surface_word_trap。D 借实验中的烟雾联想到消防系统，与主旨无关，属 wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is 'diffusion of responsibility'?

options.A: The tendency for a single witness to overreact to an emergency
options.B: The way responsibility to act spreads thin when many people are present
options.C: A method researchers use to recruit participants for experiments
options.D: The speed at which smoke spreads through a closed room

questions.explanation: 教授说当现场人多时，行动的责任被稀释、分摊得很薄，每人都以为别人会处理，B 正确。A 说单一目击者会反应过度，与讲座相反（单独时反而更易出手且不算过度），属 reversed_logic。C 把它说成招募方法，张冠李戴，属 wrong_detail。D 借用烟雾意象偷换概念，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: In the smoke experiment, how did participants behave when sitting with others who ignored the smoke?

options.A: Far fewer of them reacted to report the smoke
options.B: They reported the smoke even faster than when alone
options.C: They immediately left the room to find researchers
options.D: They opened the door to let the smoke escape

questions.explanation: 教授说当周围人对烟雾无动于衷时，出来报告的人数明显减少，A 正确。B 与讲座相反，有他人在场时反应更少而非更快，属 reversed_logic。C 说立刻离开找研究者，讲座未提且与‘更少反应’矛盾，属 wrong_detail。D 凭空添加开门放烟的行为，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the bystander effect?

options.A: It proves that most people are fundamentally callous and indifferent
options.B: It occurs because of how others reshape our sense of responsibility and reality
options.C: It disappears entirely once people receive emergency training
options.D: It affects only situations involving physical danger like fire

questions.explanation: 教授总结旁观者效应并非因人冷漠，而是他人在场悄悄改变了我们的责任感乃至对现实的判断，B 正确。A 恰是教授明确否定的解释，属 reversed_logic。C 声称受训后效应完全消失，讲座未提且过于绝对，属 over_generalization。D 把效应局限于火灾等物理危险，但讲座以更广泛情境立论，属 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: linguistics
items.topic: how creole languages emerge from pidgins
items.transcript: Picture a port town a few centuries ago, where traders, laborers, and sailors from a dozen language backgrounds all need to do business but share no common tongue. What happens? They cobble together a stripped-down communication system, borrowing words from here and there, with very simple grammar. Linguists call this a pidgin. A pidgin is nobody's native language. It's a practical tool, limited in vocabulary, and it has no fixed, elaborate grammatical rules. But here's where it gets fascinating. Suppose children grow up in that community hearing the pidgin around them. Something remarkable happens. Those children don't just learn the rough pidgin. They expand it, regularize it, and add complex, consistent grammar. Within a single generation, the pidgin becomes a full, rich language with native speakers. That new language is called a creole. The linguist Derek Bickerton studied this and argued that the grammatical patterns children add are strikingly similar across creoles worldwide, even ones that developed in complete isolation from one another. To him, that suggested children aren't just copying. They're drawing on an innate blueprint for language built into the human mind. Now, not everyone fully accepts that interpretation. But the basic pattern is widely agreed upon. So creolization gives linguists something rare, almost a live demonstration of how a fully formed language can arise, not over thousands of years, but in the space of childhood.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How traders in port towns learned each other's native languages
options.B: How a simplified pidgin develops into a full creole language
options.C: The biography and major theories of the linguist Derek Bickerton
options.D: Why some languages have simpler grammar than others

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是简化的洋泾浜语如何在一代人之内发展为成熟的克里奥尔语，B 正确。A 把话题误读成商人互学母语，与讲座所述‘他们没有共同语言、拼凑出新系统’不符，属 wrong_topic。C 只聚焦 Bickerton 的生平理论，属 too_narrow，他只是讲座中的一个引述。D 抓住‘简单语法’这个词做文章，并非主旨，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is a defining feature of a pidgin?

options.A: It is the native language of children in a port community
options.B: It has limited vocabulary and no fixed, elaborate grammar
options.C: It contains more complex grammar than any single source language
options.D: It develops gradually over many thousands of years

questions.explanation: 教授说洋泾浜语词汇有限、没有固定而精细的语法规则，B 正确。A 与讲座相反，教授强调洋泾浜语不是任何人的母语，属 reversed_logic。C 声称其语法比任何源语言都复杂，与‘语法极简’矛盾，属 wrong_detail。D 说它历经数千年才形成，但讲座强调克里奥尔化发生在一代人之内，属 topic_adjacent（混淆了时间尺度）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Derek Bickerton observe about creoles worldwide?

options.A: Their vocabularies were borrowed almost entirely from a single language
options.B: They gradually lost their grammatical complexity over time
options.C: They were eventually replaced by the original pidgins
options.D: Their grammatical patterns were strikingly similar to one another

questions.explanation: 教授引述 Bickerton 发现，即便彼此完全隔绝，各地克里奥尔语添加的语法模式也惊人地相似，D 正确。A 谈词汇来源，讲座未做此论断，属 topic_adjacent。B 说它们逐渐丧失语法复杂性，与‘儿童添加复杂一致的语法’相反，属 reversed_logic。C 称其最终被洋泾浜语取代，颠倒了发展方向，属 wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about why creolization is valuable to linguists?

options.A: It offers a rare, almost live view of how a full language can arise quickly
options.B: It proves beyond all dispute that grammar is entirely innate
options.C: It shows that fully formed languages can only develop over many centuries
options.D: It is useful mainly for teaching adults to learn foreign languages

questions.explanation: 教授说克里奥尔化给语言学家提供了罕见的、近乎实时的演示，展示成熟语言如何在童年这么短的时间里出现，A 是合理推断。B 把‘提示存在先天蓝图’夸大为‘无可争议地证明语法完全先天’，且教授特意说并非人人接受，属 over_generalization。C 与讲座相反，重点恰是语言可以快速形成，属 reversed_logic。D 把价值误导到成人外语教学，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how urban heat islands raise city temperatures
items.transcript: Have you ever noticed that a city feels hotter than the countryside just outside it, especially at night? That difference is real, and it has a name: the urban heat island effect. A city can run several degrees warmer than the surrounding rural area. So what causes it? It mostly comes down to surfaces. Think about what a city is made of. Asphalt roads, concrete buildings, dark rooftops. These materials soak up sunlight during the day and store that heat. Then, after the sun goes down, they slowly release it, keeping the city warm well into the night. The countryside, by contrast, is full of soil and plants. Plants do something clever. They release water vapor through their leaves, and that evaporation cools the air around them, a bit like how sweating cools your skin. Cities have far fewer plants and far more heat-trapping pavement. There's another piece too. All those cars, air conditioners, and machines generate waste heat of their own, adding to the warmth. Now, why does this matter? Hotter cities mean people crank up air conditioning, which uses more energy, which produces more heat. It's a cycle. But understanding the cause points to the fix. Planting more trees, adding parks, and using lighter, reflective roofing can all bring city temperatures back down.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why cities are warmer than surrounding areas and what causes it
options.B: How plants release water vapor through their leaves
options.C: Why air conditioners have become more energy efficient
options.D: How rural farming methods have changed over time

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是城市热岛效应——城市为何比周边更热及其成因，A 正确。B 只是解释乡村降温时的一个细节，属 too_narrow。C 抓住空调这个词但讲座并未讨论其能效进步，属 surface_word_trap。D 农村耕作方式与主题无关，属 wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do city surfaces keep the city warm at night?

options.A: They reflect almost all sunlight back into the sky during the day
options.B: They absorb heat during the day and slowly release it after dark
options.C: They generate water vapor that traps warmth near the ground
options.D: They block cooler air from moving in from the countryside

questions.explanation: 教授说沥青、混凝土等表面白天吸热储热，日落后缓慢释放，使城市夜间持续温暖，B 正确。A 说表面几乎全反射阳光，与‘吸收并储存热量’相反，属 reversed_logic。C 把降温的水汽错安到城市表面，混淆了乡村机制，属 wrong_detail。D 说它们阻挡乡村冷空气进入，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do plants help cool the countryside?

options.A: They absorb waste heat released by nearby machines
options.B: They reflect sunlight away with their dark leaves
options.C: They release water vapor, and evaporation cools the air
options.D: They store daytime heat and release it slowly at night

questions.explanation: 教授说植物通过叶片释放水汽，蒸发带走热量从而给空气降温，C 正确。A 说植物吸收机器废热，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。B 称深色叶片反射阳光，与讲座的蒸发降温机制不符，属 wrong_detail。D 描述的是城市铺装表面的储放热行为，张冠李戴到植物上，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the heat-and-cooling cycle in cities?

options.A: It can be eased by choices like planting trees and using reflective roofs
options.B: It makes any attempt to cool a city ultimately pointless
options.C: It affects only the largest cities and never smaller towns
options.D: It is caused mainly by people setting thermostats too low

questions.explanation: 教授指出理解成因就指向解决办法——多植树、建公园、用浅色反光屋顶都能降温，A 是合理推断。B 说降温努力终归徒劳，与教授给出可行对策相反，属 reversed_logic。C 把效应局限于最大城市，讲座未设此限，属 over_generalization。D 把成因主要归于人调低空调温度，但讲座说主因是表面材料，属 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: economics
items.topic: how network effects give some products a winner-take-all advantage
items.transcript: Why do we all seem to end up using the same few apps, even when competitors offer something just as good? The answer lies in a concept economists call the network effect. A network effect occurs when a product becomes more valuable to each user as more people use it. Think about a telephone. A single telephone is useless. Two telephones let two people talk. But a network of millions of phones is enormously valuable, because you can reach almost anyone. The value isn't in the device itself; it's in the size of the network. Now consider a social media platform. People join the one their friends are already on. And because they join, it becomes even more attractive to the next person. This creates a powerful feedback loop. Success breeds more success. Economists sometimes describe the result as winner-take-all, because one product can pull so far ahead that rivals struggle to gain a foothold, even superior ones. Here's the catch, though. This same dynamic can trap users. Once everyone's contacts and data live on one platform, switching to a competitor means leaving the network behind, so people stay put even when they're unhappy. That's called lock-in. So network effects help explain not just why certain companies grow so dominant, but why that dominance can be so remarkably hard to dislodge once it takes hold.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why early telephones failed to attract enough users
options.B: How network effects can make certain products dominant and hard to displace
options.C: How social media platforms protect their users' personal data
options.D: Why competing apps usually offer identical features

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是网络效应如何让某些产品占据主导且难以撼动，B 正确。A 只借电话作引子，并非讲座讲电话失败，属 too_narrow 且偏离。C 抓住‘数据’一词但讲座并非讲数据保护，属 surface_word_trap。D 声称竞品功能雷同，讲座只是说竞品‘同样好’，并未以此为主题，属 wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, when does a network effect occur?

options.A: When a product becomes more valuable as more people use it
options.B: When a company lowers its prices to attract new customers
options.C: When a product's value comes mainly from the device itself
options.D: When users leave a platform because their friends have left

questions.explanation: 教授定义网络效应为：随着使用人数增多，产品对每个用户更有价值，A 正确。B 把成因说成降价吸客，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。C 与讲座相反，教授强调价值不在设备本身而在网络规模，属 reversed_logic。D 描述的是用户流失，与‘越多人用越有价值’的定义方向相反，属 wrong_detail。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is 'lock-in'?

options.A: A strategy companies use to recruit their first users
options.B: The tendency for product quality to improve as networks grow
options.C: The way feedback loops attract each new user to a platform
options.D: Users staying on a platform even when unhappy, because switching means leaving the network

questions.explanation: 教授说一旦人际关系和数据都在一个平台上，转用竞品就意味着放弃整个网络，于是用户即便不满也留下，这叫 lock-in，D 正确。A 说成招募首批用户的策略，张冠李戴，属 wrong_detail。B 谈网络扩大带来质量提升，与 lock-in 无关，属 topic_adjacent。C 描述的是吸引新用户的反馈循环，而非 lock-in（留住老用户），方向不同，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a dominant product's position?

options.A: It can remain hard to dislodge even when better rivals appear
options.B: It will inevitably be overtaken by any superior competitor
options.C: It guarantees that the product will keep improving over time
options.D: It matters only for telephone and social media companies

questions.explanation: 教授指出网络效应可让一个产品远远领先，即使更优秀的对手也难以立足，故主导地位难以撼动，A 是合理推断。B 与讲座相反，教授恰恰说更好的对手也难取代，属 reversed_logic。C 把主导误推为产品必然持续改进，讲座未支持，属 over_generalization。D 把结论局限于电话和社媒，但二者只是例子，属 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: archaeology
items.topic: how dendrochronology dates ancient wooden structures
items.transcript: How do archaeologists figure out the exact year a wooden beam in an ancient building was cut, sometimes down to a single year, thousands of years later? The technique is called dendrochronology, or tree-ring dating. Here's the foundation. Every year, a tree adds a new ring of growth just under its bark. In a good year, with plenty of rain and warmth, the ring is wide. In a harsh, dry year, it's narrow. So a cross-section of a tree trunk is really a record of the climate it lived through, a sequence of wide and narrow rings unique to that span of years. Now, the clever part. Because trees in the same region all experienced the same good and bad years, their ring patterns match up. Researchers overlap samples from living trees with older and older pieces of dead wood, matching the patterns where they line up, and build a continuous timeline stretching back thousands of years. When archaeologists find an old wooden beam, they compare its ring pattern to this master timeline and find exactly where it fits. This gives a precise date. And it does more than date buildings. Because ring width reflects climate, these same records let scientists reconstruct what the weather was like centuries before any thermometer existed. So a humble tree ring turns out to be both a calendar and a climate archive. Next time, we'll look at how this method helped re-date some famous archaeological sites.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How wide tree rings differ in color from narrow ones
options.B: Why ancient buildings were constructed from wooden beams
options.C: How a master timeline of famous sites was first assembled
options.D: How tree-ring patterns are used to date wooden structures precisely

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是树木年轮断代法如何精确测定木结构的年代，D 正确。A 谈年轮颜色差异，讲座只说宽窄而非颜色，属 wrong_detail 式的 surface_word_trap。B 问古建筑为何用木梁，偏离主题，属 wrong_topic。C 聚焦‘著名遗址时间轴的组建’，那是下次内容且范围过窄，属 too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does a wide tree ring indicate?

options.A: A year with plenty of rain and warmth
options.B: A harsh, dry year with little growth
options.C: The exact year the tree was cut down
options.D: The presence of disease within the trunk

questions.explanation: 教授说在雨水充足、温暖的好年份，年轮较宽，A 正确。B 描述的是窄环对应的恶劣干旱年份，正好相反，属 reversed_logic。C 把宽环误当成砍伐年份，混淆概念，属 wrong_detail。D 说宽环表示树干有病，讲座未提，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor explain how living trees and older dead wood are overlapped?

options.A: To suggest that living trees are more accurate than dead wood
options.B: To show how a continuous timeline reaching back thousands of years is built
options.C: To describe the process of overlapping samples of wood
options.D: To argue that dead wood is the only reliable climate record

questions.explanation: 教授讲重叠匹配活树与越来越老的枯木，是为了说明如何拼出一条延伸数千年的连续时间轴，B 正确说明了目的。A 把它误解成比较活树与枯木的准确性，曲解意图，属 wrong_purpose。C 只是复述‘重叠木样’这一动作而没说明为什么，属 literal_restatement。D 拔高为‘枯木是唯一可靠的气候记录’，是教授未表达的论断，属 over_inference。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: How tree rings can serve as a record of past climate
options.B: Why trees grow wider rings in warm, wet years
options.C: How this dating method helped re-date famous archaeological sites
options.D: How thermometers were invented to measure temperature

questions.explanation: 教授结尾明确说下次将讨论该方法如何帮助重新测定一些著名考古遗址的年代，C 正确。A 是本讲已讲过的气候档案功能，属 too_narrow（停留在本讲内容）。B 也是本讲已解释的年轮宽窄成因，属 too_narrow。D 把‘温度计’这一顺带提及的词拼成下一个话题，讲座并无此预告，属 wrong_topic。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how Japanese woodblock prints influenced European modern art
items.transcript: So picture a young painter in Paris in the 1870s, unwrapping a parcel of goods shipped from Japan, and finding the wrapping paper itself covered with vivid printed images. That actually happened, and those scraps of paper helped spark a movement. The phenomenon is called Japonisme — the influence of Japanese art, especially woodblock prints, on European painters in the late nineteenth century. These prints, known as ukiyo-e, came from a totally different visual tradition. Instead of the deep, realistic space European artists had pursued since the Renaissance, ukiyo-e used flat areas of bold color, strong outlines, and unusual, off-center compositions. A figure might be cropped right at the edge of the frame, the way we'd crop a snapshot today. Now, consider Vincent van Gogh. He collected hundreds of these prints, and he even copied several directly, translating their flat color and dark outlines into his own paintings. Edgar Degas borrowed those tilted, asymmetrical viewpoints for his scenes of dancers and bathers. And Mary Cassatt adopted the prints' simple lines and patterned surfaces. What these artists took wasn't a subject but a way of seeing. They learned they didn't have to imitate three-dimensional reality. The flat surface of the canvas could be honored as a flat surface. That idea — that a painting is an arrangement of shapes and colors, not a window onto the world — became one of the foundations of modern art. So a stack of foreign prints, almost discarded as packing material, ended up reshaping the whole direction of Western painting.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Japanese woodblock prints shaped European modern art
options.B: The techniques used to produce ukiyo-e woodblock prints
options.C: Why van Gogh decided to collect art from Japan
options.D: The trade routes that carried goods from Japan to Paris

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括了整篇讲座的核心：日本浮世绘版画如何影响欧洲现代艺术（Japonisme）。B只讲版画的制作技术，讲座几乎没涉及，属于too_narrow。C只聚焦van Gogh收藏这一个细节，不是主旨，属于too_narrow。D提到的贸易路线只是开头包装纸的背景，讲座并未展开，且借用了'shipped from Japan'的字面词，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what visual qualities characterized ukiyo-e prints?

options.A: Deep realistic space and carefully blended shading
options.B: Flat areas of bold color and off-center compositions
options.C: Tiny repeated figures arranged in symmetrical rows
options.D: Muted earth tones with precise mathematical perspective

questions.explanation: 正确答案B直接来自讲座：浮世绘使用'flat areas of bold color, strong outlines, and unusual, off-center compositions'。A描述的'deep, realistic space'恰恰是欧洲文艺复兴传统，与浮世绘相反，属于reversed_logic。C的'symmetrical rows'与讲座所说的'asymmetrical/off-center'相反，且'tiny repeated figures'未提及，属于wrong_detail。D的'mathematical perspective'同样是欧洲传统而非浮世绘特征，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor say Edgar Degas borrowed from the prints?

options.A: The practice of copying entire prints stroke by stroke
options.B: Simple lines and richly patterned decorative surfaces
options.C: Tilted, asymmetrical viewpoints for his figure scenes
options.D: Bright complementary colors for painting sunlit landscapes

questions.explanation: 正确答案C来自讲座原文：Degas借用了'tilted, asymmetrical viewpoints for his scenes of dancers and bathers'。A的'copying entire prints'是讲座中描述van Gogh的做法，张冠李戴，属于wrong_detail。B的'simple lines and patterned surfaces'是讲座中Mary Cassatt采用的，也是张冠李戴，属于wrong_detail。D的'sunlit landscapes/complementary colors'讲座完全没提，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the importance of ukiyo-e for modern art?

options.A: It mattered mainly because the prints were rare and valuable
options.B: It taught European artists better techniques for realistic depth
options.C: Its influence was limited to a handful of French painters
options.D: It helped free painting from the goal of imitating reality

questions.explanation: 正确答案D是合理推断：讲座结尾强调艺术家'didn't have to imitate three-dimensional reality'，并说'a painting is an arrangement of shapes and colors, not a window onto the world'成为现代艺术基础，即帮助绘画摆脱模仿现实的目标。A的'rare and valuable'与讲座相反——版画几乎是被当作包装纸丢弃的，属于reversed_logic。B说教会'realistic depth'恰恰相反，浮世绘是反对深度写实的，属于reversed_logic。C说影响仅限少数法国画家，但讲座说它'reshaping the whole direction of Western painting'，把影响缩小了，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: neuroscience
items.topic: how mirror neurons relate to empathy and imitation
items.transcript: Have you ever winced when you saw someone else stub their toe? You felt a flash of their pain, even though nothing happened to you. That reaction points to something fascinating in the brain called mirror neurons. A mirror neuron is a brain cell that fires both when you perform an action and when you simply watch someone else perform that same action. So the same cells light up whether you grab a cup or just see a friend grab one. These neurons were discovered by accident in the 1990s. A team in Italy was recording from the brains of macaque monkeys, studying cells that activated when a monkey reached for food. One day a researcher reached for some food himself, and the monkey's neurons fired — even though the monkey hadn't moved at all. The cell responded to the observed action just as if the monkey had done it. Researchers think this mirroring system helps explain two important human abilities. First, imitation: by internally mirroring what we see, we can copy and learn new skills, which is how children pick up so much just by watching. Second, empathy: when we see someone in pain or joy, mirror neurons may let us partly feel what they feel, giving us a kind of built-in bridge into another person's experience. Now, I should add a note of caution. The role of mirror neurons is still debated, and we shouldn't claim they explain everything about human social life. But they offer a compelling clue about how our brains might be wired for connection.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How monkeys are trained to reach for food in experiments
options.B: Mirror neurons and their possible link to imitation and empathy
options.C: Why some people feel more pain than others do
options.D: The complete history of brain research in the 1990s

questions.explanation: 正确答案B准确概括主旨：镜像神经元及其与模仿和共情的可能联系，这正是讲座反复讨论的内容。A只抓住了猴子实验这一个细节，且实验只是引出概念的例子，属于too_narrow。C的'为什么有人比别人更痛'讲座从未讨论，属于wrong_topic。D说'1990年代脑研究的完整历史'夸大了，讲座只提到一次偶然发现，借用了'1990s'的字面词，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what defines a mirror neuron?

options.A: A cell that only fires when a person feels physical pain
options.B: A cell found exclusively in the brains of macaque monkeys
options.C: A cell that fires both when you act and when you watch others act
options.D: A cell that strengthens muscles during repeated movement

questions.explanation: 正确答案C是讲座给出的定义：镜像神经元'fires both when you perform an action and when you simply watch someone else perform that same action'。A把它限定为只对疼痛反应，但疼痛只是共情的一个例子，属于wrong_detail。B说只存在于猕猴大脑，但讲座明确讨论人类能力，属于wrong_detail。D的'强化肌肉'是运动生理的概念，讲座未提，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the moment a researcher reached for food?

options.A: To argue that monkeys are smarter than people assume
options.B: To warn students about errors in early brain experiments
options.C: To suggest the discovery should not have been trusted
options.D: To illustrate how mirror neurons were discovered by accident

questions.explanation: 正确答案D准确说明目的：这个研究者伸手拿食物的瞬间是用来说明镜像神经元'discovered by accident'的过程。A把焦点错误地放在'猴子更聪明'上，讲座没有这个论点，属于wrong_purpose。B说是为了警告早期实验的错误，但讲座并未把它当作错误，属于over_inference。C说暗示这个发现不该被信任，过度解读了，讲座反而把它当作重要发现，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: Why does the professor add a 'note of caution' near the end?

options.A: To remind students that the neurons' role is still debated
options.B: To explain exactly how mirror neurons produce empathy
options.C: To prove that mirror neurons do not really exist
options.D: To list the names of researchers who study the topic

questions.explanation: 正确答案A说明目的：教授加这句提醒是为了指出镜像神经元的作用'is still debated'，不应夸大。B说是为了解释镜像神经元如何产生共情，但'note of caution'恰恰是在淡化而非解释机制，属于wrong_purpose。C说为了证明镜像神经元不存在，过度引申，教授只是说作用有争议，不是否定存在，属于over_inference。D说是为了列出研究者名字，讲座此处没有列名字，属于literal_restatement的误用（描述错误内容）。

items.subject: history
items.topic: the role of coffee houses in the European Enlightenment
items.transcript: Think about where you'd go today to debate a big idea with strangers. In late seventeenth and eighteenth century Europe, the answer was often a coffee house. These establishments became unexpectedly central to the intellectual movement we call the Enlightenment. Coffee itself was new to Europe then, and crucially, it was a stimulant rather than an intoxicant. Unlike the taverns that served ale and wine, a coffee house kept its patrons alert and talkative, not drowsy. For the price of a single cup, almost anyone could enter, sit down, and join a conversation. Some London coffee houses earned the nickname 'penny universities,' because that penny bought you access to discussion, debate, and the latest pamphlets and newspapers left on the tables. What made them so important was the mixing they allowed. A merchant, a scientist, a poet, and a clergyman might share the same bench and argue as equals, at least within those walls. Ideas about politics, philosophy, and natural science circulated fast. In fact, certain coffee houses became known for particular subjects — one might attract men of science, another stock traders, another writers. Lloyd's of London, the famous insurance market, actually grew out of a coffee house frequented by merchants and ship owners. Of course, not everyone welcomed this. Authorities sometimes viewed coffee houses with suspicion, worried they were breeding grounds for criticism of the government. And that worry tells us something. These were not just places to drink a beverage; they were engines of conversation that helped a new culture of reasoned, open debate take hold across Europe.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How coffee was first imported and traded across Europe
options.B: Why taverns gradually lost customers to coffee houses
options.C: The role coffee houses played in Enlightenment intellectual life
options.D: The founding and growth of Lloyd's of London insurance

questions.explanation: 正确答案C概括主旨：咖啡馆在启蒙运动知识生活中扮演的角色，这是全篇主线。A只讲咖啡的进口与贸易，讲座没有展开，属于wrong_topic。B把焦点放在酒馆失去顾客上，讲座只是用酒馆做对比，并未讨论顾客流失，属于too_narrow。D只抓住Lloyd's这一个例子，不是主旨，属于too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did coffee suit intellectual gatherings better than ale or wine?

options.A: It was far cheaper than any alcoholic drink available
options.B: It could only be afforded by wealthy, educated patrons
options.C: It was produced locally and so was easy to obtain
options.D: It was a stimulant that kept patrons alert rather than drowsy

questions.explanation: 正确答案D来自讲座：咖啡'was a stimulant rather than an intoxicant'，让顾客'alert and talkative, not drowsy'，因此比酒更适合讨论。A说比酒便宜，讲座没有做这种价格比较（只说一便士可入场），属于wrong_detail。B说只有富有受教育者才喝得起，与'almost anyone could enter'相反，属于reversed_logic。C说咖啡本地生产、容易获得，但讲座说咖啡'was new to Europe'，属于topic_adjacent的错误推断。

questions.stem: What does the professor say the nickname 'penny universities' referred to?

options.A: A penny granted access to discussion, debate, and reading material
options.B: Universities that charged students only a penny for tuition
options.C: A tax of one penny that the government placed on coffee
options.D: Coffee houses that were physically located inside universities

questions.explanation: 正确答案A来自讲座原文：'penny universities'指那一便士'bought you access to discussion, debate, and the latest pamphlets and newspapers'。B把它理解成真正收一便士学费的大学，望文生义，属于wrong_detail。C说是政府对咖啡征收一便士税，讲座未提此税，属于topic_adjacent。D说咖啡馆设在大学里，与讲座意思无关，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What can be inferred from the fact that authorities viewed coffee houses with suspicion?

options.A: Coffee houses were eventually shut down all across Europe
options.B: The open debate in coffee houses could challenge those in power
options.C: Most customers went there only to read official announcements
options.D: Authorities preferred that people gather in taverns instead

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断：当局担心咖啡馆是'breeding grounds for criticism of the government'，说明那里的开放辩论可能挑战当权者。A说咖啡馆最终在全欧被关闭，讲座没有这个信息，属于over_generalization。C说顾客主要去读官方公告，与讲座强调的辩论、批评相反，属于reversed_logic。D说当局更希望人们去酒馆，讲座没有这种比较或暗示，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: how light refraction produces a rainbow
items.transcript: After a rainstorm, when the sun comes out behind you, you might catch a rainbow arcing across the sky. It looks like a band painted on the clouds, but it's really the result of sunlight playing tricks inside millions of tiny raindrops. Here's the key idea: white sunlight is actually a mixture of all the colors, and those colors bend by different amounts when they pass into water. This bending of light as it moves from one material into another is called refraction. So follow a single ray of sunlight into a spherical raindrop. As the ray enters the drop, it refracts, or bends. It travels to the back of the drop, reflects off the inner surface, and then refracts again as it exits. Because each color bends at a slightly different angle, the colors fan out and separate. Red light bends the least, so it ends up on the outer edge of the bow, while violet bends the most and lands on the inner edge. Now here's why a rainbow is curved rather than a straight line. Your eye only catches the separated light coming from drops at one particular angle — about forty-two degrees from the point directly opposite the sun. All the drops sitting at that angle form a circle around that point, and that's the arc you see. This also explains something curious: no two people see exactly the same rainbow, because each observer's eye receives light from a different set of raindrops. The rainbow, in a sense, belongs to you alone.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why rainstorms are usually followed by sunny weather
options.B: How raindrops form and fall from clouds in the sky
options.C: The cultural meanings people have attached to rainbows
options.D: How refraction inside raindrops produces a rainbow

questions.explanation: 正确答案D概括主旨：折射如何在雨滴内部形成彩虹，这是全篇核心。A说雨后为何放晴，讲座只是用雨后场景作引子，未讨论天气原因，属于wrong_topic。B说雨滴如何形成下落，讲座讲的是光在雨滴里的行为，不是雨滴的形成，属于wrong_topic。C说彩虹的文化含义，讲座完全是物理解释，属于wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to a ray of sunlight inside a raindrop?

options.A: It refracts entering, reflects off the back, then refracts leaving
options.B: It passes straight through without bending or reflecting at all
options.C: It is completely absorbed and converted into heat energy
options.D: It splits only because the raindrop spins as it falls

questions.explanation: 正确答案A来自讲座对光路的描述：光线进入时折射，在背面反射，离开时再次折射。B说光直穿不弯不反射，与讲座完全相反，属于reversed_logic。C说光被完全吸收变成热，讲座说光是被折射分离出来的，属于wrong_detail。D说颜色分离是因为雨滴旋转，讲座说是因为不同颜色折射角度不同，与旋转无关，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the angle of about forty-two degrees?

options.A: To prove that violet light bends more sharply than red light
options.B: To explain why the rainbow appears as a curved arc
options.C: To state that raindrops are exactly forty-two degrees apart
options.D: To show that rainbows can only form at sunrise or sunset

questions.explanation: 正确答案B说明目的：提到约42度这个角度是为了解释为什么彩虹是弯曲的弧形——位于该角度的所有雨滴形成一个圆。A说是为了证明紫光比红光弯曲更厉害，那是另一个细节，与42度的作用无关，属于wrong_purpose。C把它当作字面陈述说雨滴相距42度，曲解了角度的含义，属于literal_restatement。D说为了表明彩虹只在日出日落形成，讲座没有这个说法，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply when saying the rainbow 'belongs to you alone'?

options.A: Rainbows are an optical illusion that does not really exist
options.B: A person can move to make a rainbow follow them precisely
options.C: Each observer sees light from a different set of raindrops
options.D: Only one person at a time is able to see any given rainbow

questions.explanation: 正确答案C是讲座明示的推断依据：'no two people see exactly the same rainbow, because each observer's eye receives light from a different set of raindrops'。A说彩虹是不存在的幻觉，过度引申，讲座说彩虹是真实的光学现象，属于reversed_logic。B说人可以移动让彩虹精确跟随，讲座没有这个意思，属于topic_adjacent。D说一次只有一个人能看到彩虹，把'每人看到的不同'夸大成'只有一人能看到'，属于over_generalization。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how honeybees use the waggle dance to communicate food locations
items.transcript: Imagine you've just found a great restaurant, and you want to tell a friend exactly where it is — but you're not allowed to speak or point. How would you do it? Honeybees face this very problem, and they've solved it with something remarkable: the waggle dance. When a forager bee discovers a rich patch of flowers, she returns to the dark interior of the hive and performs a dance on the vertical surface of the honeycomb. The dance has a distinctive figure-eight shape, with a straight run through the middle where the bee waggles her body from side to side. That straight run is the part that carries the information. The direction of the waggle run, relative to straight up, encodes the direction of the food relative to the sun. If the bee runs straight up, the food lies toward the sun; if she angles thirty degrees to the right, the food is thirty degrees to the right of the sun. And the duration of the waggle tells the others how far away the food is — a longer waggle means a greater distance. This was decoded by Karl von Frisch, who spent decades patiently observing marked bees, and it earned him a Nobel Prize. What's striking is that this is symbolic communication. The bee isn't simply leading others to the food; she's transmitting abstract information about direction and distance that her hive-mates then use to fly there themselves. For a long time, scientists assumed language-like communication was unique to humans. The waggle dance forced them to think again.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How honeybees produce honey inside the hive
options.B: How bees communicate food locations through a dance
options.C: Why some flower patches attract more bees than others
options.D: How Karl von Frisch trained bees to follow commands

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括主旨：蜜蜂如何通过舞蹈传达食物位置，这是全篇核心。A说蜜蜂如何产蜜，讲座完全没讲产蜜过程，属于wrong_topic。C说为何某些花丛吸引更多蜜蜂，讲座没讨论花丛吸引力的差异，属于wrong_topic。D说von Frisch训练蜜蜂服从命令，但讲座说他只是观察标记过的蜜蜂、解码舞蹈，并非训练，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the duration of the waggle run indicate?

options.A: The distance to the food source
options.B: The exact species of flower that was found
options.C: The number of other bees needed to collect it
options.D: The direction of the food relative to the sun

questions.explanation: 正确答案A来自讲座：'the duration of the waggle tells the others how far away the food is'，即摆动持续时间表示距离。B说表示花的种类，讲座未提花种，属于topic_adjacent。C说表示需要多少蜜蜂去采集，讲座没有这个信息，属于topic_adjacent。D说表示食物相对太阳的方向，但那是由摆动'方向'编码的，不是'持续时间'，混淆了两个变量，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the direction of the straight run encode?

options.A: The richness of the flower patch the bee discovered
options.B: The direction of the food relative to the position of the sun
options.C: How long the food source is expected to last
options.D: The time of day when the bee first left the hive

questions.explanation: 正确答案B来自讲座：摆动直线段的方向'encodes the direction of the food relative to the sun'。A说编码花丛的丰富程度，讲座没说舞蹈传达食物质量，属于topic_adjacent。C说编码食物能维持多久，讲座未提，属于topic_adjacent。D说编码蜜蜂离巢的时间，与讲座所述方向信息无关，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply by calling the waggle dance 'symbolic communication'?

options.A: The dance conveys abstract information, not just a trail to follow
options.B: The dance is mainly a ritual with no real practical function
options.C: Only specially trained bees are able to understand the dance
options.D: The dance works exactly the same way as human spoken language

questions.explanation: 正确答案A是讲座明确支持的推断：教授说蜜蜂'isn't simply leading others to the food; she's transmitting abstract information about direction and distance'，这正是'symbolic'的含义。B说舞蹈只是没有实际功能的仪式，与讲座说它真正传递可用信息相反，属于reversed_logic。C说只有受过训练的蜜蜂才懂，讲座没有这种限定，属于topic_adjacent。D说舞蹈与人类口语完全一样，夸大了，讲座只是说它促使科学家重新思考人类语言的独特性，并未等同，属于over_generalization。

items.subject: economics
items.topic: how the tragedy of the commons explains overuse of shared resources
items.transcript: Picture a pasture open to everyone in a village, where any herder may graze as many cattle as they like. Now, what happens to that shared field over time? This scenario is the heart of a famous idea in economics called the tragedy of the commons, popularized by Garrett Hardin in 1968. The logic runs like this. Each herder gains the full benefit of adding one more animal to the pasture — that's a whole extra cow they own. But the cost, the wear on the shared grass, is spread across everyone who uses the field. So from any single herder's point of view, adding another animal always seems rational. The trouble is, every herder reasons the same way. Animals keep getting added, the grass is grazed faster than it can regrow, and eventually the pasture is destroyed — ruined for all of them. The key insight is that individually rational decisions can add up to a collectively disastrous outcome. And this isn't only about cattle. The same pattern shows up with overfished oceans, polluted air, and depleted groundwater — any resource that's shared and freely available. Now, Hardin's framing has been challenged. The political scientist Elinor Ostrom showed that real communities often invent rules, quotas, and social agreements to manage shared resources successfully, without simply privatizing them or calling in the government. So the tragedy isn't inevitable. But it explains why, without some form of cooperation or regulation, shared resources are so often overused and run down.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why shared resources are often overused, and how that can happen
options.B: How villages in the past organized their farming and grazing
options.C: The life and academic career of the scholar Garrett Hardin
options.D: Why privatizing land is always the best economic policy

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括主旨：公共资源为何常被过度使用以及其内在逻辑，这正是'公地悲剧'讲座的核心。B说过去村庄如何组织农耕放牧，讲座只是用牧场作比喻，不讨论农业史，属于wrong_topic。C说Hardin的生平与学术生涯，讲座只提他在1968年提出这一观点，属于too_narrow。D说私有化土地永远是最佳政策，讲座反而通过Ostrom说明私有化不是唯一出路，属于over_generalization的错误主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why does each herder keep adding more animals?

options.A: The herder gets the full benefit while the cost is shared by all
options.B: The herder is required by village law to expand the herd
options.C: Adding animals makes the grass regrow noticeably faster
options.D: The herders have agreed in advance to share every animal

questions.explanation: 正确答案A来自讲座的核心逻辑：每个牧人独享多养一头牛的全部收益，而成本（草地损耗）由所有人分摊，所以总觉得多养是理性的。B说村法要求扩大牛群，讲座没有这种规定，属于topic_adjacent。C说加牛会让草长得更快，与讲座所说'grazed faster than it can regrow'相反，属于reversed_logic。D说牧人事先约定共享每头牛，与讲座'a whole extra cow they own'相反，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor say Elinor Ostrom demonstrated?

options.A: That the tragedy of the commons is mathematically impossible
options.B: That only government control can protect shared resources
options.C: That communities can devise their own rules to manage resources
options.D: That Hardin had invented the idea of the commons himself

questions.explanation: 正确答案C来自讲座：Ostrom证明真实社区常常'invent rules, quotas, and social agreements to manage shared resources successfully'。A说她证明公地悲剧在数学上不可能，讲座没说不可能，只说不是必然，属于wrong_detail。B说只有政府管制才能保护资源，与讲座'without simply privatizing them or calling in the government'相反，属于reversed_logic。D说Hardin自己发明了公地概念，讲座说他是'popularized'这一观点，且这与Ostrom无关，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the tragedy of the commons?

options.A: It can be avoided when people cooperate or set up rules
options.B: It only ever applies to grazing cattle on open pasture
options.C: It proves that human beings can never act unselfishly
options.D: It happens regardless of whether a resource is shared

questions.explanation: 正确答案A是合理推断：讲座说Ostrom证明社区能成功管理资源，并明确'the tragedy isn't inevitable'，即通过合作或规则可以避免。B说它只适用于公共牧场放牛，但讲座举了过度捕捞、空气污染、地下水枯竭等例子，属于too_narrow式的over_generalization反向缩小。C说它证明人永远无法无私，过度引申，讲座只说个体理性可能导致集体灾难，没说人不能无私，属于over_generalization。D说无论资源是否共享都会发生，与讲座强调'shared and freely available'相反，属于reversed_logic。

items.subject: linguistics
items.topic: how children acquire grammar through overgeneralization errors
items.transcript: Listen to a three-year-old for a while and you'll hear something odd. A child who once correctly said 'I went to the park' suddenly starts saying 'I goed to the park.' It sounds like a step backward. But linguists see it as a sign of remarkable progress. This pattern is called overgeneralization, and it's a window into how children acquire grammar. Here's what's going on. English has a regular rule for the past tense: add '-ed.' Walk becomes walked, jump becomes jumped. Young children first learn common irregular verbs like 'went' or 'ate' as whole, separate words, simply by hearing and memorizing them. But at some point, the child detects the underlying '-ed' rule. And once they've grasped that rule, they begin applying it everywhere — even to the irregular verbs they used to get right. So 'went' becomes 'goed,' and 'ate' becomes 'eated.' Notice what this tells us. The child was never taught to say 'goed.' No adult models that word. The child must be generating it from an internal rule they worked out on their own. This is strong evidence that children aren't simply imitating the speech around them; they're actively constructing a grammar, forming hypotheses about how the language works. Over the next couple of years, they gradually relearn the exceptions, and the errors fade. So when a child says 'goed,' it isn't a failure. It's the sound of a young mind discovering a rule.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why some English verbs are irregular in the past tense
options.B: What children's grammar errors reveal about learning language
options.C: How parents should correct their children's speech mistakes
options.D: Which words three-year-olds learn earliest and most easily

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括主旨：儿童的语法错误揭示了他们如何习得语言，这是全篇核心。A说为何英语某些动词不规则，讲座只是用不规则动词作例子，主旨不在动词为何不规则，属于too_narrow。C说父母该如何纠正孩子，讲座没有给纠正建议，属于wrong_topic。D说三岁儿童最早学会哪些词，讲座借用了'three-year-old'的字面词，但没讨论最早学的词表，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do children first learn irregular verbs like 'went'?

options.A: By memorizing them as whole, separate words
options.B: By applying the regular '-ed' rule to them
options.C: By being explicitly corrected by adults each time
options.D: By reading them repeatedly in printed books

questions.explanation: 正确答案A来自讲座：孩子最初把'went''ate'等不规则动词'as whole, separate words, simply by hearing and memorizing them'。B说通过对它们套用-ed规则，但那是后来过度泛化阶段才发生的，时间顺序错了，属于reversed_logic。C说靠成人每次明确纠正，讲座强调没人教也没人示范，属于wrong_detail。D说靠反复阅读印刷书籍，讲座说是靠听，三岁孩子也不靠读书，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor point out that no adult models the word 'goed'?

options.A: To suggest that adults speak carelessly around young children
options.B: To show the child must be generating the word from an internal rule
options.C: To recommend that adults avoid using irregular verbs with children
options.D: To note that the word 'goed' sounds similar to the word 'went'

questions.explanation: 正确答案B说明目的：强调没有成人示范'goed'，是为了证明孩子一定是从自己总结的内部规则生成这个词，而非模仿。A说为了暗示成人说话粗心，讲座没有这个意思，属于wrong_purpose。C说为了建议成人不要对孩子用不规则动词，讲座没有给这种建议，属于over_inference。D说只是指出'goed'与'went'读音相似，那是在复述无关的表面信息，并非教授的真实目的，属于literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about children learning language?

options.A: They simply copy the sentences they hear from adults
options.B: They learn grammar faster when adults correct every error
options.C: They actively work out the rules of language themselves
options.D: They cannot master irregular verbs until adulthood

questions.explanation: 正确答案C是讲座明确支持的推断：教授说孩子'aren't simply imitating'，而是'actively constructing a grammar, forming hypotheses'，即主动自己推导规则。A说孩子只是照搬听到的句子，与讲座结论正好相反，属于reversed_logic。B说成人纠正每个错误能让学得更快，讲座没有这个因果关系，属于topic_adjacent。D说孩子要到成年才能掌握不规则动词，但讲座说错误在随后几年就消退，属于over_generalization。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how urban heat islands form and why cities are warmer than rural areas
items.transcript: Here's something you can test yourself. On a summer evening, drive from the countryside into the center of a city, and watch a car thermometer climb several degrees, even though you've traveled only a few miles. That temperature gap has a name: the urban heat island effect. It describes how built-up urban areas tend to be noticeably warmer than the rural land around them, especially at night. Why does this happen? A big part of the answer is materials. Cities are paved with asphalt and concrete, and these dark, dense surfaces absorb a great deal of solar energy during the day. Then, after sunset, they slowly release that stored heat. Natural landscapes behave very differently. Soil and vegetation hold water, and as that water evaporates, it carries heat away and cools the surroundings — a process called evapotranspiration. A city of concrete has very little of that cooling moisture. There are other contributors too. Tall buildings trap heat in narrow streets and block breezes, and cars, air conditioners, and factories pump out additional warmth. The consequences are real. Heat islands drive up demand for air conditioning, which strains the power grid, and they can make heat waves more dangerous for city residents. But the effect can be reduced. Planting trees, creating parks, and installing 'green roofs' or lighter-colored, reflective pavement can all help bring city temperatures down. Understanding why the heat builds up in the first place is what points us toward those solutions.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why cities become warmer than surrounding rural areas
options.B: How car thermometers measure outdoor temperature changes
options.C: Why summer evenings are hotter than summer mornings
options.D: How air conditioning technology has improved over time

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括主旨：城市为何比周边乡村更热（城市热岛效应），这是全篇核心。B说车载温度计如何测温，讲座只是用它作引子，不讨论测温原理，属于surface_word_trap。C说夏夜为何比夏晨热，讲座没有比较一天内不同时段，属于wrong_topic。D说空调技术如何进步，讲座只把空调当作产热与耗电的因素之一，属于too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do asphalt and concrete contribute to the heat island effect?

options.A: They reflect almost all sunlight back into the sky immediately
options.B: They absorb solar energy by day and release it after sunset
options.C: They hold large amounts of water that slowly evaporates
options.D: They block sunlight so completely that streets stay cool

questions.explanation: 正确答案B来自讲座：沥青和混凝土'absorb a great deal of solar energy during the day'，日落后'slowly release that stored heat'。A说它们几乎把阳光立即反射回天空，与'吸收大量太阳能'相反，属于reversed_logic。C说它们储存大量水分慢慢蒸发，那是土壤植被的行为，讲座说混凝土城市缺少这种水分，张冠李戴，属于wrong_detail。D说它们完全挡住阳光让街道凉爽，与热岛效应相反，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention evapotranspiration?

options.A: To explain how natural landscapes cool themselves, unlike concrete cities
options.B: To argue that cities should stop using any concrete at all
options.C: To define a technical term that students must memorize
options.D: To claim that evaporation is the only cause of heat islands

questions.explanation: 正确答案A说明目的：提到蒸散作用是为了解释自然地表如何通过水分蒸发自我降温，从而对比缺少水分的混凝土城市。B说为了主张城市应完全停用混凝土，讲座没有这种极端建议，属于over_inference。C说只是为了定义一个学生必须背的术语，那是把'介绍术语'当作目的本身，忽略了它的对比功能，属于literal_restatement。D说为了断言蒸发是热岛唯一成因，但讲座明确还列了高楼、汽车、空调等其他因素，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the urban heat island effect?

options.A: It is an unavoidable consequence of any city's existence
options.B: It can be lessened through deliberate design choices
options.C: It affects rural areas more strongly than urban ones
options.D: It has no real impact on residents' daily lives

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断：讲座说种树、建公园、装绿色屋顶或浅色反光路面'can all help bring city temperatures down'，说明通过有意的设计选择可以减轻热岛效应。A说它是任何城市无法避免的必然结果，与讲座给出的缓解办法相反，属于over_generalization。C说它对乡村影响更强，与讲座说城市比乡村更热相反，属于reversed_logic。D说它对居民日常生活没有真正影响，与讲座说它加剧热浪危险、增加用电负担相反，属于reversed_logic。

items.subject: history
items.topic: how the printing press changed the spread of scientific ideas
items.transcript: When we picture a scientific revolution, we tend to imagine a brilliant individual—a Copernicus, a Galileo—working alone. But the spread of new scientific ideas in early modern Europe depended on a piece of technology: Johannes Gutenberg's printing press, developed around 1440. Before printing, scientific knowledge circulated in hand-copied manuscripts. A single copy could take a scribe months to produce, and errors crept in with every copying. So a diagram of the human body or a star chart might be wildly inaccurate by its tenth copy. The press changed that. Suddenly, hundreds of identical copies could be produced quickly and cheaply. And here's the part people overlook: it wasn't just speed. It was accuracy and standardization. When an astronomer in Italy and an astronomer in England could look at the exact same printed diagram, with the exact same numbers, they could actually compare results and build on each other's work. Consider Andreas Vesalius, who published his anatomy text in 1543. Its detailed, reproducible illustrations let physicians across the continent study identical images of human anatomy. That simply wasn't possible before. Printing also let ideas spread faster than authorities could suppress them. By the time a controversial claim was banned in one city, copies were already circulating in a dozen others. So the printing press didn't just preserve knowledge—it created a shared, stable foundation that scientists everywhere could trust and challenge together. In a real sense, the scientific community as we know it was built on the printed page.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the printing press transformed the spread of scientific ideas
options.B: The life and discoveries of astronomers like Copernicus and Galileo
options.C: The techniques scribes used to copy manuscripts by hand
options.D: Why anatomy became a leading science in early modern Europe

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。整篇讲座的核心是印刷术如何改变科学思想的传播——通过速度、准确性和标准化，建立了科学界共享的基础。B项（哥白尼、伽利略的生平）只是开头一句话提到的引子，不是主题，属于too_narrow。C项（抄写员手抄技术）只是用来对比印刷前的状况，是细节而非主旨，属于too_narrow。D项（解剖学成为主流科学）误用了讲座中维萨里的例子，但讲座并未说解剖学成为领先科学，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what was a problem with hand-copied scientific manuscripts?

options.A: They were written only in Latin, so few people could read them
