Think of a dark parking lot baking in the afternoon sun. These surfaces then release that stored heat slowly, well into the night, which is why cities stay warm after dark. Vegetation makes a difference too. In the countryside, plants and soil release water vapor, a process that cools the air, much like sweating cools your body. Cities, with far less greenery, lose that natural cooling. And then there's waste heat. Cars, air conditioners, and factories all pump heat directly into the air. Now, this isn't just a matter of comfort. Higher city temperatures increase the demand for air conditioning, which raises energy use and, ironically, generates even more waste heat. It can also worsen air pollution and pose health risks during heat waves. Researchers are exploring solutions, like planting more trees, creating green roofs, and using lighter-colored, more reflective building materials. So a city's temperature isn't just about the weather. It's shaped by the very design of the city itself.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why cities are warmer than surrounding rural areas and what causes it
options.B: How air conditioning systems function in large modern buildings
options.C: The health risks that heat waves pose to elderly city residents
options.D: Why the countryside receives more rainfall than nearby cities

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括全篇——城市为何比周边乡村更热以及其成因，即城市热岛效应，这是讲座定义、成因和解决方案始终围绕的主题。B是too_narrow，空调只是产生废热的一个因素，并非主题。C是too_narrow，热浪健康风险只是结尾一笔带过的后果之一。D是wrong_topic，讲座提到乡村植物蒸发降温，但并未讨论降雨量多少。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do cities stay warm after dark?

options.A: Building materials release stored heat slowly into the night
options.B: Plants in the city release large amounts of water vapor
options.C: Rural areas send their warm air toward nearby cities
options.D: Streetlights and signs generate most of the city's heat

questions.explanation: 正确答案A对应讲座原文——沥青、混凝土等材料白天储热，'release that stored heat slowly, well into the night'。B是reversed_logic，植物释放水汽是降温过程，且城市绿化少，与让城市夜间变暖相反。C是topic_adjacent，听上去合理，但讲座从未说乡村把暖空气送往城市。D是wrong_detail，讲座列举的废热来源是汽车、空调和工厂，并未说路灯和广告牌是主要热源。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare plant water vapor to sweating?

options.A: To explain how releasing water vapor cools the surrounding air
options.B: To argue that the countryside is more humid than the city
options.C: To describe how human bodies regulate their temperature
options.D: To suggest that plants need a great deal of water to survive

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授用'出汗降温'这个类比来帮助听者理解植物释放水汽如何冷却周围空气。B是over_inference，讲座没有比较城乡湿度。C是literal_restatement，仅复述了出汗这一表面内容，没有解释类比的真正目的。D是wrong_purpose，类比的重点是降温机制，而非植物的需水量。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about air conditioning in cities?

options.A: It is the single largest cause of the urban heat island
options.B: It can make the urban heat island problem worse
options.C: It has little effect on a city's overall temperature
options.D: It cools cities enough to offset heat from other sources

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说高温增加空调需求，'raises energy use and, ironically, generates even more waste heat'，可推断空调会使热岛问题更严重。A是over_generalization，讲座说最大因素是建筑材料，而非把空调说成单一最大成因。C是reversed_logic，讲座暗示空调显著加剧升温，并非影响甚微。D是reversed_logic，空调向空气排放废热使城市更热，而非降温抵消其他热源。

items.subject: linguistics
items.topic: how children acquire grammar without being explicitly taught the rules
items.transcript: Think about a four-year-old child. She's never had a grammar lesson, she can't tell you what a verb or a subject is, and yet she produces sentences that follow remarkably complex rules. How is that possible? This puzzle sits at the heart of the study of language acquisition. Children acquire grammar not by memorizing rules, but by unconsciously extracting patterns from the speech they hear around them. And here's the striking evidence: they make errors that no adult ever models for them. A child will say 'I goed to the park' or 'I have two foots.' Now, nobody talks like that. The child has never heard an adult say 'goed.' So where does it come from? The answer is that the child has internalized a rule, namely, that you add an '-ed' to make the past tense, and is applying it consistently, even to irregular verbs. Linguists call this overregularization. It's actually a sign of intelligence, not error. The child isn't parroting. She's generating language from a rule she worked out herself. This observation led the linguist Noam Chomsky to argue that humans are born with an innate capacity for language, a kind of mental blueprint for grammar. The input children receive, he pointed out, is often too messy and incomplete to fully explain how quickly and uniformly they learn. So rather than being blank slates, children may come equipped with a built-in framework that guides them. The errors, paradoxically, reveal the hidden machinery of the mind at work.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How adults can best teach grammar rules to young children
options.B: How children come to follow grammar rules without being taught them
options.C: Why irregular verbs are the hardest part of learning English
options.D: The biography and major theories of the linguist Noam Chomsky

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括全篇——孩子如何在无人明确教授的情况下掌握语法规则，这是讲座从谜题、过度规则化证据到乔姆斯基理论始终围绕的核心。A是reversed_logic，讲座恰恰强调孩子并非被大人教会，而是自己提取规律。C是too_narrow，不规则动词只是说明过度规则化的例子，不是主旨。D是too_narrow，乔姆斯基只是被引用来支持论点的一位学者，讲座并非他的传记。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do children say things like 'goed' or 'foots'?

options.A: They are copying errors they hear adults make
options.B: They have applied a regular rule to an irregular word
options.C: They are unable to remember words they hear
options.D: They are deliberately playing with sounds for fun

questions.explanation: 正确答案B对应讲座解释——孩子内化了'加-ed构成过去式'的规则，并一致地套用到不规则动词上，即过度规则化。A是reversed_logic，讲座明确说'nobody talks like that'，孩子从未听大人这样说，并非模仿。C是wrong_detail，讲座说孩子是在自己生成规则，而非记不住单词。D是topic_adjacent，听上去合理，但讲座说这是套用规则的结果，并非故意玩声音。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say that overregularization is 'a sign of intelligence, not error'?

options.A: To emphasize that the child is generating language from a rule she worked out
options.B: To argue that children are smarter than adults at learning language
options.C: To point out that the child has simply memorized many new words
options.D: To show that such mistakes will disappear as the child grows older

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授强调过度规则化是智力的体现，是为了说明孩子并非鹦鹉学舌，而是依据自己推导出的规则生成语言。B是over_inference，讲座没有比较孩子和成人谁更聪明。C是literal_restatement并曲解，讲座恰恰说孩子不是死记硬背，而是运用规则。D是over_inference，讲座此处未谈这些错误是否会随年龄消失。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about Chomsky's view of children's minds?

options.A: Children learn language mainly by carefully imitating adult speech
options.B: Children are born with a built-in framework that guides language learning
options.C: Children must be explicitly taught grammar to learn it correctly
options.D: Children's language ability depends mostly on how much input they get

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授引述乔姆斯基的观点，认为孩子并非白板，而是'come equipped with a built-in framework that guides them'，即天生具备引导语言学习的内在框架。A是reversed_logic，讲座强调孩子不是单纯模仿大人。C是reversed_logic，讲座的核心正是孩子无需被明确教授就能掌握语法。D是topic_adjacent并被反驳，乔姆斯基恰恰指出输入'too messy and incomplete'，不足以解释学习，因此能力不主要取决于输入量。

items.subject: economics
items.topic: why people value something more once they own it (the endowment effect)
items.transcript: Imagine I give you a coffee mug, just a plain one, for free. A few minutes later, I offer to buy it back from you. How much would you ask for? Now imagine instead that you never received the mug, and I simply ask how much you'd pay to buy one. Studies show something surprising. The people who own the mug typically demand about twice as much to sell it as the people without one are willing to pay. The mug hasn't changed. Only ownership has. This is what economists call the endowment effect. The endowment effect is the tendency for people to value something more highly simply because they own it. It was demonstrated famously by Daniel Kahneman and his colleagues in a series of experiments in the 1990s, often using exactly this kind of mug. Why does this happen? A big part of the explanation is something called loss aversion. Psychologically, losing something feels worse than gaining the equivalent thing feels good. So once the mug is yours, giving it up registers as a painful loss, and you demand a higher price to compensate. Notice that this challenges a basic assumption in traditional economics, the idea that an object has a stable value independent of who holds it. The endowment effect shows that value is partly psychological, shaped by ownership and the fear of loss. This has practical implications, from how companies offer free trials to how we cling to possessions we'd never actually choose to buy again.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How companies design free trial offers for new products
options.B: The experiments Daniel Kahneman conducted in the 1990s
options.C: A tendency to value things more highly once we own them
options.D: Why coffee mugs are commonly used in economic research

questions.explanation: 正确答案C概括全篇——人们一旦拥有某物就会更看重它的倾向，即禀赋效应，这是讲座定义、实验和理论含义始终围绕的主题。A是too_narrow，免费试用只是结尾提到的一个实际应用。B是too_narrow，Kahneman的实验只是支撑主题的证据之一。D是surface_word_trap，借用了反复出现的'mug'，但杯子只是实验道具，并非讲座主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did studies find about mug owners?

options.A: They demanded about twice as much to sell as buyers would pay
options.B: They refused to sell the mug at any price at all
options.C: They valued the mug exactly as much as non-owners did
options.D: They were willing to pay more than non-owners to buy one

questions.explanation: 正确答案A对应讲座原文——拥有杯子的人开出的卖价'about twice as much'于无杯者愿意支付的买价。B是wrong_detail，讲座说他们要价更高，并非拒绝以任何价格出售。C是reversed_logic，讲座的核心正是拥有者比非拥有者更看重，而非估值相同。D是wrong_detail，混淆了买卖双方——是拥有者的卖价高，而非他们更愿出钱去买。

questions.stem: Why does the professor explain the concept of loss aversion?

options.A: To explain why owning something makes giving it up feel painful
options.B: To prove that people enjoy gains more than they fear losses
options.C: To describe how Kahneman first discovered the coffee mug study
options.D: To argue that all economic decisions are based on emotion

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授引入损失厌恶是为了解释为何一旦拥有某物，放弃它会被感知为痛苦的损失，从而支撑禀赋效应。B是reversed_logic，损失厌恶恰恰指失去比获得更痛苦，与此选项相反。C是wrong_purpose，损失厌恶用于解释机制，与如何发现杯子实验无关。D是over_inference，教授说价值'partly psychological'，并未主张所有经济决策都基于情绪。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the traditional economic view of value?

options.A: It correctly predicts how owners price their possessions
options.B: It is incomplete because value is partly shaped by ownership
options.C: It has been fully replaced by the concept of loss aversion
options.D: It applies only to inexpensive everyday objects like mugs

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说禀赋效应'challenges'传统经济学关于价值稳定、与持有者无关的假设，可推断该传统观点并不完整，因为价值部分受拥有权影响。A是reversed_logic，传统观点恰恰无法解释拥有者要价更高的现象。C是over_generalization，讲座说'challenges'而非'完全取代'，损失厌恶是解释禀赋效应的原因之一。D是topic_adjacent，讲座没有说传统价值观只适用于廉价日常物品。
